main_ingredients: Kolhapuri masala (dried red chilies, coriander, cumin, black pepper, cloves, cinnamon, cardamom, poppy seeds, coconut)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mutton Kolhapuri is approximately 250 g, comprising bone-in or boneless mutton pieces in a thick, fiery Kolhapuri masala gravy, usually accompanied by roti, naan, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Kolhapuri — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as sourced from HPB Foodo. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable mutton curry preparations (approx. 4.5 g/100 g); this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on slow-cooked lamb/mutton curry with mixed spices and vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Kolhapuri in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 30 is estimated by analogy with slow-cooked lamb/mutton curry entries (GI range 25–35) from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 18.6) / 100 ≈ 5.6, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 7.44 × 2.5 = 18.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100 g with low potassium (210 mg/100 g) and very low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g), insufficient to offset sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to ≤100 g and request reduced salt in masala preparation, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1168 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — while potassium and fibre levels are too low to provide meaningful cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (7.44 g/100 g) and sugar (4.69 g/100 g) with high protein (14.05 g/100 g); low estimated GI (~30) supports moderate post-meal glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Kolhapuri has a low glycaemic index (~30) and moderate carbohydrate content; individuals with type 2 diabetes may consume a controlled portion (≤150 g) paired with a low-GI accompaniment such as cauliflower rice or brown rice, while monitoring total meal carbohydrate load and avoiding high-GI sides such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.5 g/100 g estimated), cholesterol (85 mg/100 g), and sodium (467 mg/100 g) are all elevated; mutton is red meat with arachidonic acid content promoting pro-inflammatory pathways
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Kolhapuri to occasional consumption (≤100 g), as a 250 g serving provides approximately 11.25 g saturated fat and 212 mg cholesterol alongside 1168 mg sodium, all of which contribute to atherogenic risk; substituting with lean chicken or fish curry is advisable for regular meals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (red meat, high arachidonic acid), nightshade ingredients (tomato, dried red chilies), and elevated sodium (467 mg/100 g) all exacerbate RA inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Kolhapuri due to its combination of arachidonic acid-rich red meat, nightshade vegetables (tomato, dried red chilies), and high sodium, all of which amplify TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways and worsen joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Kolhapuri is a fiery, slow-cooked Indian mutton curry originating from Kolhapur, a city in Maharashtra, western India, renowned for its intensely spiced cuisine. The dish is prepared by marinating bone-in or boneless mutton pieces in a robust Kolhapuri masala — a complex spice blend comprising dried red Kashmiri and Bedgi chilies, coriander, cumin, black pepper, cloves, cinnamon, cardamom, poppy seeds, and desiccated coconut — then slow-braising the meat with tomatoes, onions, garlic, and ginger until the mutton is tender and the gravy is thick, dark red, and deeply aromatic. The flavour profile is intensely spicy, earthy, and slightly smoky, with a rich umami depth from the slow-rendered mutton fat and toasted spices. The texture is tender and succulent with a thick, coating gravy. Per 100 g, Mutton Kolhapuri provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Sodium is elevated at 467 mg/100 g, making it a concern for hypertension management. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index (~30) due to its high protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. Clinically, it is high concern for cardiovascular disease and hypertension due to saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium, and moderate concern for type 2 diabetes when portion-controlled. It is a popular dish in Indian restaurants across Singapore, commonly served with roti canai, naan, or steamed rice at lunch and dinner.

Mutton Kolhapuri vs Mutton Rogan Josh

confidence_reason: All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN comparable mutton curry data and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable slow-cooked mutton curry preparations, as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat for this entry; this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN value when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Mutton Kolhapuri preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. Cultural classification corrected from 'Middle Eastern' (input error) to 'Indian' — Kolhapuri cuisine originates from Kolhapur, Maharashtra, India. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on slow-cooked lamb/mutton curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for Mutton Kolhapuri exists in approved sources. 5. Halal status is assumed based on mutton being a halal-permissible meat; actual halal certification depends on the specific restaurant or preparation.
